perturbation.original_text: No party may directly or indirectly make an of fer employment to, or request an interest from an employee or agent of the other party , for a least 1 (one) year of signing this agreement and for at least 1 (one) year after its termination.
perturbation.changed_text: No party may directly or indirectly make an of fer employment to, or request an interest from an employee or agent of the other party , for at least 5 (five) years of signing this agreement and for at least 5 (five) years after its termination.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 5 years could be deemed unreasonably restrictive and may violate South African labour laws prohibiting unreasonable restraints of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 22 of the South African Constitution guarantees the right to freedom of trade, occupation, and profession. The modified text, by imposing a five-year non-solicitation restriction both during the agreement and after termination, potentially infringes on this right by unduly limiting the employment opportunities of employees or agents. A five-year ban is much greater than what a court may find reasonable, thus making it unenforceable and possibly violating the spirit of Section 22.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The change from a 1-year non-solicitation period to a 5-year period creates a legal contradiction because it potentially violates Section 22 of the South African Constitution, which guarantees the right to freedom of trade, occupation, and profession. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this extension could be unreasonably restrictive, while the Law Explanation suggests a five-year ban is likely unenforceable and infringes on employment opportunities. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', the explanation provided indicates the changed text may not be within legal restrictions.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties must deliver all legal documents, notices or other communications to the addresses provided and indicated as domicilium citandi et executandi:
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties must deliver all legal documents, notices or other communications to the addresses provided and indicated as domicilium citandi et executandi. All correspondence must be written in English.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring all correspondence to be in English might be seen as discriminatory, and may violate the South African constitution which recognizes 11 official languages.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 6 of the South African Constitution recognizes 11 official languages. While English is one of them, requiring all legal documents to be exclusively in English potentially violates the rights of individuals or entities who prefer to communicate in another official language, thus creating an unfair barrier to accessing legal remedies or fulfilling contractual obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 18 - CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE 18 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 18 - CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I - CRIMES CHAPTER 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 6 - Department and agency defined From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §6. Department and agency defined As used in this title: The term “department” means one of the executive departments enumerated in section 1 of Title 5, unless the context shows that such term was intended to describe the executive, legislative, or judicial branches of the government. The term “agency” includes any department, independent establishment, commission, administration, authority, board or bureau of the United States or any corporation in which the United States has a proprietary interest, unless the context shows that such term was intended to be used in a more limited sense. (June 25, 1948, ch. 645, 62 Stat. 685.) Historical and Revision Notes This section defines the terms “department” and “agency” of the United States. The word “department” appears 57 times in title 18, U.S.C., 1940 ed., and the word “agency” 14 times. It was considered necessary to define clearly these words in order to avoid possible litigation as to the scope or coverage of a given section containing such words. (See United States v. Germaine , 1878, 99 U.S. 508, 25 L. Ed. 482, for definition of words “department” or “head of department.”) The phrase “corporation in which the United States has a proprietary interest” is intended to include those governmental corporations in which stock is not actually issued, as well as those in which stock is owned by the United States. It excludes those corporations in which the interest of the Government is custodial or incidental. References in Text Section 1 of Title 5, referred to in text, was repealed by Pub. L. 89–554, §8, Sept. 6, 1966, 80 Stat. 632, and reenacted by the first section thereof as section 101 of Title 5, Government Organization and Employees.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a requirement for all correspondence to be in English, which the LLM explanation states, potentially violates the rights of individuals or entities who prefer to communicate in another official language, creating an unfair barrier. While the U.S.C. Title 18 snippet is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation, coupled with the Perturbation Explanation, and the original text recognizing the right to communicate in any language, makes it clear that the contract has a legal contradiction.
